content.full_text:
CITY OF Riverside OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY
3900 Main Street • Riverside, California 92522 • 714-782-5567
Telecopier 714-782-5540

DEC 21 9 54 AM '90

JOHN WOODHEAD
City Attorney

December 21, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
Conflict of Interest Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Dear Sir or Madam:

The undersigned is City Attorney of the City of
Riverside and this letter is written on behalf of Terry
Frizzel, Mayor of the City of Riverside.

Mayor Frizzel has an ownership interest in a house
where she resides located approximately 600 feet from the
boundaries of a large parcel of property known as La Sierra
Ranch. The La Sierra Ranch property is located within a
larger territory, comprising a number of different
ownerships, covered by a highly restrictive zoning ordinance
adopted by an initiative known as Proposition C.

The owners of La Sierra Ranch have filed an action
in U.S. District Court against the City and its City
Council, including Mayor Frizzel, alleging that they have
been deprived of federal constitutional rights as a result
of the City's refusal to process a subdivision they have
proposed on their property which calls for greater density
than allowed by the current zoning.

Pursuant to the suggestion of the federal judge
presiding over the case, the parties have discussed
settlement and in that connection, the owners of La Sierra
Ranch have participated in an on-going general plan
amendment study in progress. They have suggested a specific
plan for this property. The Mayor serves as member of the
City Council subcommittee on land use. Under the City
Charter she only has the right to vote on specific matters
to break a tie and also has the power to veto any matter
which is then subject to an override by the members of the
City Council.

The owners of the La Sierra Ranch contend that Mayor
Frizzel should be disqualified from participating in any way

Fair Political Practices Commission    -2-    December 21, 1990


in this process due to the proximity of her home to their
property and the possibility that her home's value may be
affected in an amount of more than $10,000.  The owners of
the La Sierra Ranch have also acquired an interest in
another parcel of property covered by Proposition C which is
located within 300 feet of Mayor Frizzel's home.  The owners
have contended that Mayor Frizzel is opposed to any
suggestions they have made because she is concerned that any
proposed development would impair the view from her home.

The questions posed are:

     1.  May Mayor Frizzel vote or otherwise participate
in the process of amending the general plan as it relates to
the La Sierra Ranch?

     2.  May Mayor Frizzel vote or otherwise participate
in any specific plan proposed for the development of the
La Sierra Ranch?

     3.  Is either or both of the above questions
impacted by the fact that Mayor Frizzel is a defendant in a
federal action challenging the validity of the land use
controls imposed upon the La Sierra Ranch and may have to
approve further litigation or settlement of such action?

     For further background we attach a letter from
Attorney Roger Brown and request that you contact the
undersigned or Chief Assistant City Attorney, Norman
Herring, or the City's special counsel, Richard Terzian of
Adams, Duque and Hazeltine, 523 West Sixth Street, Los
Angeles, California 90014 (telephone number 213-620-1240),
if you desire additional background information.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        JOHN WOODHEAD
                                        City Attorney

JW/6709L/n

Attachment

cc:  Richard Terzian, Esq.

SEP 28 '90 :5:30 HAUN                                                P.1

                              Law Offices of
                         LAWRENCE A. HAUN
                    Eleven and One-half South Washington Street
Lawrence A. Haun              Post Office Box 1056              RECEIVE
Roger A. Brown                Sonora, California 95370
                    Telephone (209) 532-1958    Telefopier (209) 532-1963
                                                                 SEP 2 8
                                                            CITY ATTORNEY

                              September 28, 1990


     The Honorable Teresa R. Frizzel
     Mayor of The City of Riverside
     11290 Arlington Avenue
     Riverside, CA 92505

          Re: Possible Conflict of Interest

     Dear Mayor Frizzel:

          This is to advise you of a possible conflict of interest on
     your part and to request that you disqualify yourself from
     participating in any way in decisions or deliberations concerning
     certain property (the Subject Property) which is more
     particularly described in Exhibit A to this letter and
     incorporated herein by this reference.

          The owners of the Subject Property intend to request the
     City to commence processing a plan of devalopmnt for the Subject
     Property. This proposal will include processing certain
     entitlements which would include but are not limited to a
     specific plan, a tentative map and a development agreement.

          You are required to disqualify yourself because any decision
     on these matters could foreseeably result in a material financial
     effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally
     on you or real property in which you have a direct or indirect
     interest worth $1,000 or more. (Government Code Sections 87100,
     87103.) Section 87100 specifically provides:

               No public official at any level of State or
          local government shall make, participate in making
          or in any way attempt to use his official position
          to influence a governmental decision in which he
          knows or has reason to know he has a financial
          interest.

          You own real property at 1129 Arlington Avenue, Riverside.
     Your property is directly across the street and within 300 feet
     of a portion of the tract of land covered by the proposed
     development. The plan would, among other things, permit the
     development of a golf course and upscale residences across the
     street from your residence.

SEP 28 '90 15:31 HAUN                                P.2


The Honorable Teresa R. Frizzel
September 28, 1990
Page 2


     The law presumes the effect of the decision would be
foreseeable and material on real property situated less than 300
feet from any part of the property which is the subject of the
decision. (2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18702.2(a)(1).) This
regulation, promulgated by the Fair Political Practices
Commission, states in relevant part:

     (a) The effect of a decision is material as
     to real property in which the official has a
     direct, indirect or beneficial ownership
     interest . . . if any of the following applies:

          (1) The real property in which the official has an
     interest, or any part of that real property, is located
     within a 300 foot radius of the boundaries (or proposed
     boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
     decision, unless the decision will have no financial
     effect upon the official's real property interest.
     (copy enclosed.)

     The FPPC has repeatedly advised public officials to
disqualify themselves from decisions similar to the one before
the Riverside City Council in this matter. For example, in the
Height Advice Letter No. A-88-432, the Commission disqualified a
City Councilmember from decisions concerning a project consisting
of several hundred acres, 265 dwelling units and an 18-hole golf
course. The councilman's residence was located on the property
bordering the proposed project.

     The Commission concluded that the councilman must disqualify
himself, stating:

          Since one of the boundaries of the proposed
          project borders Councilman Caffrey's
          residential property, pursuant to subdivision
          (a)(1) of Regulation 18702.3, the decision
          to approve and certify the EIR will have a
          material financial effect on Councilmember
          Caffrey's real property interest. . .

          Councilmember Caffrey's residential property
          adjoins the proposed development project. In
          this respect, the councilmember's residence
          is situated differently from most residential
          property and Scotts Valley. Thus, the effect
          on his property is distinguishable from its
          effect on the public generally. Accordingly,
          Councilmember Caffrey must disqualify himself

SEP 28 '90 15:31 HAUN                                                P.3


                    The Honorable Teresa R. Frizzel
                    September 28, 1990
                    Page 3


                    from participating in the decision to approve
               and certify the EIR.

                    Disqualification would be required even if your property was
               more than 300 feet from the Subject Property. The effect of a
               decision will be material on real property within 2,500 feet of
               the project area when it is reasonably foreseeable that the fair
               market value of your property could be affected by $10,000 or
               more. (2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18702.3(a)(3), copy enclosed.)
               The regulation lists such considerations on the
               characterization of the neighborhood, traffic, view, privacy, intensity
               of use, noise levels, air emissions and other things in
               determining whether the fair market value could reasonably be
               affected by $10,000 or more. It is irrelevant whether the effect
               is to increase or decrease the value.

                    In this case for example, the golf course, bridle trails and
               upscale residences will affect the view, the character of the
               neighborhood, and the median value of homes-in-the-area. It-is
               reasonably foreseeable, considering all relevant factors, that a
               decision on the entitlement or development of this subject
               property will have an effect on the fair market value of your
               real property of $10,000 or more. As a result, even if your
               property was more than 300 feet away, you would still be
               required to disqualify yourself.

                    We are providing this information and analysis to you and
               the City Attorney to assist you in preventing and avoiding a
               conflict of interest violation of the Political Reform Act. We
               do not wish to make a public issue of this nor do we want to
               embarrass the City or any public official. We are confident
               that, upon reflection, you will see the clear conflict and take
               the appropriate voluntary action to disqualify yourself.

                    We encourage you to consult the City Attorney, FPPC,
               District Attorney or Attorney General to confirm our analysis.
               We believe that competent counsel familiar with conflict of
               interest law will advise you to disqualify yourself.

                    It is important to obtain sound advice in this area because
               the penalties for violation of the conflict of interest
               provisions of the Political Reform Act can be severe. For
               example, it is a crime to knowingly or willfully violate any
               provision of the Act. In addition to fines and imprisonment, the
               law provides for disqualification from running for office for a
               period of four years following conviction.

                    There are other penalties which may be imposed by civil suit
               or administrative enforcement action by the FPPC.

SEP 28 '90 15'32 HAUN                                                P.4


The Honorable Teresa R. Frizzel
September 28, 1990
Page 4


     Please notify this office at once whether you intend to
disqualify yourself from participating in any decisions
concerning the entitlement or development of the Subject Property
so that we may determine whether any further action is required
to protect our clients' interests.

     Please note that you must disqualify yourself on the record,
stating the disqualifying financial interest and abstaining from
participating in or attempting to influence the decision. (2
Cal.Code Regs. Section 18700(b)(5), copy enclosed.) This means
that you may not vote on the decision, make or second a motion,
engage in debate, ask questions or otherwise take part in the
decision-making process. (Johnson Advice Letter No. A-85-500; 2
Cal.Code Regs. Section 18700.1(a) copy enclosed.) You would
also be prohibited from privately discussing these matters with
other members of the city Council or with other city officials.
(Kilian Advice Letter No. A-89-522, copy enclosed.)

     If the Council considers the entitlement or development of
the Subject Property in closed session, you are prohibited from
attending or obtaining a recording or transcript of the meeting
relating to your area of conflict. (Hamilton v. Town of Los
Gatos (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1050.)

     For all of the reasons stated herein, you should voluntarily
disqualify yourself.

     Thank you very much for your prompt attention to this very
important matter.

                                        Yours truly,

                                        [signature]

                                        Roger A. Brown


EAS/ru:218.002

Enclosures

cc:  City Attorney

RIVER RANCH PROPERTY

EXHIBIT A

21 ACRES ■ Sexion Property

110 ACRES ■ Tyler Street Property Tract 240i

54 ACRES ■ Savi

487 ACRES ■ Arlington Group

46 ACRES ■ Diaz Property

ALASKAN ENGINEERS
INTERNATIONAL INC.
ANCHORAGE - FAIRBANKS

SEP 28 '90 15:33 HAUN                                                P.5
